title: Welcome to my Personal Site & AI Geoscience Hub
summary: Welcome to my new personal portfolio and blog. In this post, I introduce the website design, outline my professional background, and explain how the automated weekly AI geoscience paper aggregator works.
full_content:
Welcome to my personal site and blog! This space serves as both my professional portfolio and a hub for keeping track of the rapid developments at the intersection of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and the Geosciences.

Why this site?

As a Senior Software and Data Engineer at Predictia, I spend my days building robust pipelines for climate data workflows, orchestrating containerized services in operational environments, and building deep learning models for meteorology. Climate data is inherently large, complex, and high-dimensional (working with NetCDF, GRIB, and Zarr formats).

In recent years, the integration of Machine Learning (ML) in weather and climate modeling has exploded. Models like AIFS (ECMWF), FourCastNet, GraphCast, and various regional emulators are transforming how we predict weather and assess climate projections.

Keeping up with these papers can be overwhelming. To address this, I built an automated aggregator into this blog.

How the Paper Aggregator Works

Every week, an automated script queries the public arXiv API for papers matching specific machine learning search terms in five key domains:

  1. Global & Regional Weather Forecasting
  2. Subseasonal to Seasonal (S2S) Forecasting
  3. Climate Emulation
  4. Data Assimilation
  5. Downscaling

The fetched papers are categorized, merged with any manual blog posts I write, and updated directly on this page. You can filter posts using the tags in the blog section.

Open Source & Collaboration

I'm a big believer in open science and open source. Many of my research topics and codebases are hosted publicly. For instance:

  • aq-biascorrection: Bias correction of CAMS air quality forecasts using PyTorch. GitHub Repo
  • DeepR: Regional downscaling of global reanalysis datasets. GitHub Repo

Feel free to look around, explore the papers, and get in touch if you have any questions or collaborations in mind!

title: Universal Diffusion-Based Probabilistic Downscaling
summary: We introduce a universal diffusion-based downscaling framework that lifts deterministic low-resolution weather forecasts into probabilistic high-resolution predictions without any model-specific fine-tuning.
full_content: We introduce a universal diffusion-based downscaling framework that lifts deterministic low-resolution weather forecasts into probabilistic high-resolution predictions without any model-specific fine-tuning. A single conditional diffusion model is trained on paired coarse-resolution inputs (~25 km resolution) and high-resolution regional reanalysis targets (~5 km resolution), and is applied in a fully zero-shot manner to deterministic forecasts from heterogeneous upstream weather models. Focusing on near-surface variables, we evaluate probabilistic forecasts against independent in situ station observations over lead times up to 90 h. Across a diverse set of AI-based and numerical weather prediction (NWP) systems, the ensemble mean of the downscaled forecasts consistently improves upon each model's own raw deterministic forecast, and substantially larger gains are observed in probabilistic skill as measured by CRPS. These results demonstrate that diffusion-based downscaling provides a scalable, model-agnostic probabilistic interface for enhancing spatial resolution and uncertainty representation in operational weather forecasting pipelines.
explanation:
Downscaling is the telescope of weather forecasting. While global models provide the "big picture" of atmospheric circulation, local decisions—like crop management, wind turbine configurations, or storm preparation—require detailed micro-level inputs. Imagine trying to plan flood defenses for a city based on a forecast that averages the weather across an entire county; local terrain, coastlines, and urban structures create microclimates that are completely lost in coarse global models. Historically, meteorologists have bridged this gap by running expensive regional models or applying simple statistical corrections. This paper introduces a breakthrough: a model-agnostic, zero-shot downscaling framework powered by a conditional diffusion model. It lifts deterministic coarse-resolution weather forecasts into probabilistic high-resolution predictions without needing to retrain or adjust for different upstream weather systems.

## The Core Problem

Traditional numerical weather prediction (NWP) models, such as the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) Integrated Forecasting System (IFS), operate at coarse horizontal resolutions (typically 9 to 25 kilometers). The physical constraints are simple: doubling the resolution of a global grid requires an eight-fold increase in computing power, making global high-resolution simulation computationally impossible on operational timescales.

To obtain local predictions at scales of 5 kilometers or finer, meteorologists employ two main strategies:
1. **Dynamical Downscaling**: Running a regional model (like WRF) nested within the global forecast. This is computationally expensive, slow, and still prone to regional biases.
2. **Statistical Downscaling**: Using historical observations to map coarse forecasts to fine resolutions. Standard statistical methods struggle to represent non-linear relationships, and deterministic deep learning models output a single "average" prediction. This averaging effect strips away the variance and uncertainty (probabilities) crucial for storm risk assessments, extreme wind events, or temperature extremes.

Furthermore, existing machine learning downscaling models are tied to a specific upstream model. If the global forecast model changes, the downscaler must be completely retrained from scratch on years of paired historical data. There is a critical need for a universal downscaler that is model-agnostic and produces well-calibrated probabilistic ensembles.

## The Model and Approach

The proposed framework utilizes a conditional denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM). Diffusion models function by learning to reverse a process that gradually adds noise to a high-resolution target image. In this weather application, the diffusion model is conditioned on coarse-resolution (~25 km) atmospheric fields to reconstruct realistic, high-resolution (~5 km) regional weather fields.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Input Coarse Resolution
        Coarse[Coarse Forecast: ~25km]
    end
    subgraph Denoising Process
        Noise[Gaussian Noise] --> Diff[Conditional Diffusion Model]
        Coarse -->|Conditioning Input| Diff
        Diff -->|Iterative Denoising| HR_Sample[High-Resolution Sample: ~5km]
    end
    subgraph Probabilistic Output
        HR_Sample -->|Run N Times| Ensemble[Probabilistic Ensemble Forecast]
    end
```

The core innovation is that the diffusion model is trained on historical reanalysis data paired with high-resolution regional reanalysis. It maps general coarse-resolution states (like those from ERA5) to high-resolution regional reanalysis (like COSMO-REA6). At inference time, the model operates in a fully **zero-shot** manner. It can take low-resolution forecasts from entirely unseen upstream systems—including physical NWP models (IFS, GFS) and machine learning emulators (GraphCast, AIFS)—and generate realistic, fine-scale probabilistic forecasts without any model-specific fine-tuning. By running the denoising process multiple times with different initial noise seeds, it generates a full ensemble of possible high-resolution outcomes, capturing the true uncertainty of the forecast.

## Key Results and Benchmarks

The universal downscaling framework was rigorously evaluated against independent in-situ station observations over lead times up to 90 hours. The evaluation focused on near-surface parameters, including 2-meter temperature and 10-meter wind speed, which are heavily influenced by local topography.

When tested across a diverse set of upstream systems, the diffusion-based downscaler achieved outstanding results:
* **Deterministic Skill**: The ensemble mean of the downscaled forecasts consistently outperformed the raw deterministic inputs from the upstream models. It significantly reduced Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) by recovering localized features like valley temperatures or coastal wind gradients.
* **Probabilistic Skill**: Evaluated using the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS), the downscaled forecasts demonstrated massive gains. The generated ensembles were highly calibrated, showing that the spread of the ensemble matched the actual error of the forecast.
* **Model Agnosticism**: The downscaler worked successfully on both conventional physics-based models and machine learning emulators, maintaining high downscaling quality without requiring model-specific tuning or adjustments.

These results prove that diffusion-based downscaling can serve as a plug-and-play probabilistic interface, enhancing resolution and uncertainty representation across operational weather forecasting pipelines.

## Key Takeaways
- **Zero-Shot Generalization**: A single conditional diffusion model downscales forecasts from heterogeneous upstream models (NWP or ML) without retraining.
- **Resolution Leap**: Scales coarse-resolution inputs (~25 km) to regional high-resolution forecasts (~5 km) while preserving physical consistency.
- **Probabilistic Calibration**: Generates well-calibrated ensembles that represent localized weather uncertainty, outperforming raw forecasts on CRPS.
- **Computational Efficiency**: Running the diffusion model takes seconds per ensemble member, avoiding the massive computational overhead of dynamical regional models.

title: AERIS: Argonne Earth Systems Model for Reliable and Skillful Predictions
summary: Generative machine learning offers new opportunities to better understand complex Earth system dynamics. Recent diffusion-based methods address spectral biases and improve ensemble calibration in weather forecasting compared to deterministic methods, yet have so far proven difficult to scale stably at high resolutions. We introduce AERIS, a 1.3 to 80B parameter pixel-level Swin diffusion transformer to address this gap...
full_content: Generative machine learning offers new opportunities to better understand complex Earth system dynamics. Recent diffusion-based methods address spectral biases and improve ensemble calibration in weather forecasting compared to deterministic methods, yet have so far proven difficult to scale stably at high resolutions. We introduce AERIS, a 1.3 to 80B parameter pixel-level Swin diffusion transformer to address this gap, and SWiPe, a generalizable technique that composes window parallelism with sequence and pipeline parallelism to shard window-based transformers without added communication cost or increased global batch size. On Aurora (10,080 nodes), AERIS sustains 10.21 ExaFLOPS (mixed precision) and a peak performance of 11.21 ExaFLOPS with 1×1 patch size on the 0.25° ERA5 dataset, achieving 95.5% weak scaling efficiency, and 81.6% strong scaling efficiency. AERIS outperforms the IFS ENS and remains stable on seasonal scales to 90 days, highlighting the potential of billion-parameter diffusion models for weather and climate prediction.
explanation:
Weather forecasting and climate projection are undergoing a massive transition from classical physics-based models to generative artificial intelligence. Generative models, particularly diffusion-based models, have shown exceptional skill in addressing spectral biases (the tendency of models to predict overly smooth weather fields) and generating well-calibrated ensemble forecasts. However, scaling these generative models to high resolutions and massive parameter sizes has proven to be an engineering and computational bottleneck. To bridge this gap, this paper introduces AERIS (Argonne Earth Systems Model), a pixel-level Swin diffusion transformer scaling from 1.3 to 80 billion parameters, and SWiPe, a novel parallelization technique that allows stable training of large-scale weather models on supercomputers.

## The Core Problem

As AI weather models scale, they face a double challenge: maintaining physical realism and resolving fine-scale features. While early models (like GraphCast or Pangu-Weather) produced highly accurate deterministic forecasts, their outputs tended to smooth out over time, losing small-scale atmospheric features like localized convective storms or wind gusts. Diffusion-based generative models solve this smoothing problem by treating weather prediction as a stochastic process, generating realistic fluctuations that preserve energy spectra across all scales.

However, scaling diffusion models to high-resolution global grids (like the 0.25-degree ERA5 dataset, which contains millions of grid points per variable) requires massive parameter budgets. Traditional parallelization techniques—like data parallelism or model sharding—suffer from several limits when applied to window-based spatial transformers (like Swin Transformers):
1. **Communication Bottleneck**: Window-based attention requires tokens to be grouped locally, and sharding these windows across different processors introduces massive, latency-sensitive communication overheads.
2. **Batch Size Constraints**: Increasing data parallelism scales the global batch size, which can degrade training stability and convergence speed.
3. **Memory Limits**: Storing the optimizer states and activations for an 80-billion parameter model exceeds the memory of any single GPU or node, requiring efficient model splitting.

Without a dedicated parallelization strategy, training a billion-parameter diffusion model for high-resolution global meteorology remains computationally infeasible.

## The Model and Approach

AERIS is built around a pixel-level Swin diffusion transformer architecture. Unlike standard vision transformers that process fixed-size patches (like 4x4 or 8x8 pixels), AERIS operates down to the 1x1 patch size, preserving fine-grained, high-resolution details directly. The model is conditioned on previous atmospheric states and denoises the subsequent states iteratively.

To train models containing up to 80 billion parameters, the authors developed **SWiPe** (Swin Window-based Parallelism with Sequence and Pipeline parallelism). SWiPe is a generalizable technique that composes three parallelization dimensions:

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Input Grid
        Grid[0.25° Global Grid] --> Patch[1x1 Pixel Patches]
    end
    subgraph SWiPe Parallelism
        Patch --> WindowParallel[Window Parallelism: Shard Local Attention]
        WindowParallel --> SeqParallel[Sequence Parallelism: Split Across Nodes]
        SeqParallel --> PipeParallel[Pipeline Parallelism: Partition Layers]
    end
    subgraph Model Architecture
        PipeParallel --> SwinDiff[Swin Diffusion Transformer]
        SwinDiff --> Scale[1.3B to 80B Parameters]
    end
    subgraph Output
        Scale --> StableOut[Stable 90-Day Seasonal Simulations]
    end
```

By sharding the window-based transformer without adding communication cost or increasing the global batch size, SWiPe enables efficient distributed training across thousands of compute nodes. This framework was implemented on the Aurora supercomputer, utilizing up to 10,080 nodes to scale the training of AERIS models.

## Key Results and Benchmarks

AERIS was evaluated on the 0.25-degree ERA5 dataset, with scaling and performance benchmarks conducted on the Aurora supercomputer:
* **Supercomputing Scale**: Utilizing 10,080 nodes, AERIS sustained an incredible **10.21 ExaFLOPS** (mixed precision) and achieved a peak performance of **11.21 ExaFLOPS** with 1x1 patch size.
* **Scaling Efficiency**: The model demonstrated a **95.5% weak scaling efficiency** and **81.6% strong scaling efficiency**, proving that SWiPe eliminates the communication bottlenecks typical of distributed transformers.
* **Forecast Accuracy**: AERIS outperformed the Integrated Forecasting System Ensemble (IFS ENS) from ECMWF, producing highly calibrated probabilistic forecasts.
* **Seasonal Stability**: Unlike many machine learning weather models that diverge or drift after 10-15 days, AERIS remained physically stable and realistic on seasonal scales up to **90 days**.

This work demonstrates that scaling generative diffusion models to tens of billions of parameters is not only possible but provides the stability and accuracy required for long-term climate emulation and seasonal weather forecasting.

## Key Takeaways
- **Massive Scaling**: Introduces AERIS, scaling weather-focused Swin diffusion transformers from 1.3B to 80B parameters.
- **SWiPe Parallelism**: A novel composition of window, sequence, and pipeline parallelism that shards window transformers without communication overhead.
- **Exascale Performance**: Sustains 10.21 ExaFLOPS on the Aurora supercomputer using 10,080 nodes.
- **Seasonal Stability**: Maintains stable, physically realistic forecasts up to 90 days, outperforming ECMWF IFS ENS.

title: EPT-2 Technical Report
summary: We present EPT-2, the latest iteration in our Earth Physics Transformer (EPT) family of foundation AI models for Earth system forecasting. EPT-2 delivers substantial improvements over its predecessor, EPT-1.5, and sets a new state of the art in predicting energy-relevant variables...
full_content: We present EPT-2, the latest iteration in our Earth Physics Transformer (EPT) family of foundation AI models for Earth system forecasting. EPT-2 delivers substantial improvements over its predecessor, EPT-1.5, and sets a new state of the art in predicting energy-relevant variables-including 10m and 100m wind speed, 2m temperature, and surface solar radiation-across the full 0-240h forecast horizon. It consistently outperforms leading AI weather models such as Microsoft Aurora, as well as the operational numerical forecast system IFS HRES from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF). In parallel, we introduce a perturbation-based ensemble model of EPT-2 for probabilistic forecasting, called EPT-2e. Remarkably, EPT-2e significantly surpasses the ECMWF ENS mean-long considered the gold standard for medium- to longrange forecasting-while operating at a fraction of the computational cost. EPT models, as well as third-party forecasts, are accessible via the app.jua.ai platform.
explanation:
As the global transition to renewable energy accelerates, the demands on weather forecasting systems have shifted. Grid operators, solar farmers, and wind power companies do not just need general predictions of atmospheric pressure or high-altitude winds; they require highly accurate, localized forecasts of surface temperature, solar radiation, and wind speeds at both 10-meter and 100-meter heights (hub heights for modern wind turbines). This technical report presents EPT-2, the latest iteration in the Earth Physics Transformer (EPT) family, designed specifically as a foundation model for global and regional Earth system forecasting with a strong focus on energy-relevant variables.

## The Core Problem

Standard numerical weather prediction (NWP) models and even early AI weather models (like GraphCast) are primarily optimized to minimize global atmospheric errors (such as geopotential height at 500 hPa). While this leads to excellent general weather tracking, it often results in subpar performance for variables at the boundary layer (the lowest part of the atmosphere), where human activity and renewable energy generation take place.

Variables like 100-meter wind speed and surface solar radiation are highly sensitive to local turbulence, surface roughness, and cloud cover.
1. **Local Boundary Layers**: Physics-based models parameterize these processes using empirical formulas that often fail to capture the complex, non-linear dynamics of boundary-layer physics.
2. **Computational Expense**: Producing high-resolution ensemble forecasts to quantify risk (e.g., wind ramps or sudden drops in solar power) requires running dozens of parallel model runs, which is computationally prohibitive for national meteorological services.
3. **Forecast Horizon**: Models must maintain high skill across the entire medium-range horizon (0 to 240 hours) to allow energy trading markets and grid operators to plan capacity and reserves effectively.

There is a critical need for a foundation model that can capture boundary-layer atmospheric physics and generate highly accurate, low-cost ensemble forecasts for energy-relevant variables.

## The Model and Approach

EPT-2 is a specialized transformer-based foundation model trained on massive amounts of climate and weather data, including historical ERA5 reanalysis and high-resolution operational datasets. It uses a spatial-temporal transformer architecture optimized to process multi-variable atmospheric fields and learn the physics of the boundary layer directly from data.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Data Input
        Obs[Atmospheric Observations & Reanalysis] --> EPT_Model[EPT-2 Transformer Backbone]
    end
    subgraph Specialty Output Heads
        EPT_Model --> Wind100[100m Wind Speed]
        EPT_Model --> Wind10[10m Wind Speed]
        EPT_Model --> Temp2m[2m Temperature]
        EPT_Model --> Solar[Surface Solar Radiation]
    end
    subgraph Probabilistic Ensembling
        EPT_Model -->|Perturbation-based Ensembling| EPT_2e[EPT-2e Ensemble Model]
        EPT_2e --> ProbOut[Probabilistic Energy Risk Forecasts]
    end
```

To address the need for probabilistic forecasting, the authors introduce **EPT-2e**, a perturbation-based ensemble model of EPT-2. EPT-2e generates multiple forecast members by applying small, physically consistent perturbations to the initial states, allowing the model to project a range of probable atmospheric developments. EPT-2 and EPT-2e are designed to run efficiently on GPU hardware, making high-member ensemble generation extremely fast and cheap compared to traditional NWP methods.

## Key Results and Benchmarks

The performance of EPT-2 and EPT-2e was evaluated across a 0-240 hour forecast horizon, with a specific focus on variables critical to the energy sector:
* **Energy Variables**: EPT-2 set a new state of the art in predicting 10m wind speed, 100m wind speed, 2m temperature, and surface solar radiation.
* **Benchmark Comparisons**: EPT-2 consistently outperformed leading AI models, such as Microsoft Aurora, as well as the high-resolution deterministic numerical forecast system (IFS HRES) from the ECMWF.
* **Ensemble Superiority**: The perturbation-based ensemble model, EPT-2e, significantly surpassed the ECMWF ENS mean (historically the gold standard for probabilistic medium-range forecasting) across all lead times.
* **Operational Access**: Both EPT-2 and third-party forecast datasets are hosted and accessible via the jua.ai platform, providing real-time data access for energy grid management.

These results establish EPT-2 as a leading foundation model for industrial meteorology, demonstrating that data-driven models can capture boundary-layer physical variables with greater precision than classical physics simulators.

## Key Takeaways
- **Energy-Optimized**: Designed and optimized specifically for boundary-layer variables like 100m wind speed and surface solar radiation.
- **Superior Accuracy**: Outperforms Microsoft Aurora and ECMWF IFS HRES across the 0-240h forecast horizon.
- **EPT-2e Ensemble**: Perturbation-based ensemble outskills the ECMWF ENS mean at a fraction of the computational cost.
- **API Availability**: Fully deployed and accessible via the app.jua.ai platform for commercial and research applications.

title: WeatherMesh-3: Fast and accurate operational global weather forecasting
summary: We present WeatherMesh-3 (WM-3), an operational transformer-based global weather forecasting system that improves the state of the art in both accuracy and computational efficiency. We introduce the following advances: 1) a latent rollout... and 2) a modular architecture...
full_content: We present WeatherMesh-3 (WM-3), an operational transformer-based global weather forecasting system that improves the state of the art in both accuracy and computational efficiency. We introduce the following advances: 1) a latent rollout that enables arbitrary-length predictions in latent space without intermediate encoding or decoding; and 2) a modular architecture that flexibly utilizes mixed-horizon processors and encodes multiple real-time analyses to create blended initial conditions. WM-3 generates 14-day global forecasts at 0.25-degree resolution in 12 seconds on a single RTX 4090. This represents a >100,000-fold speedup over traditional NWP approaches while achieving superior accuracy with up to 37.7% improvement in RMSE over operational models, requiring only a single consumer-grade GPU for deployment. We aim for WM-3 to democratize weather forecasting by providing an accessible, lightweight model for operational use while pushing the performance boundaries of machine learning-based weather prediction.
explanation:
The integration of machine learning in meteorology has shown that deep learning models can outperform traditional physics-based models in weather forecasting accuracy. However, a major bottleneck remains: deployment cost and accessibility. Many state-of-the-art AI weather models (like GraphCast) contain hundreds of millions of parameters and require specialized multi-GPU server clusters to run inference. This compute barrier prevents smaller meteorological centers, researchers, and local organizations from using these models. This paper presents WeatherMesh-3 (WM-3), an operational transformer-based global weather forecasting system that achieves a 100,000-fold speedup over traditional numerical weather prediction (NWP) models, enabling a 14-day global weather forecast to run in just 12 seconds on a single consumer-grade RTX 4090 GPU.

## The Core Problem

Traditional Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) models rely on supercomputers to solve fluid dynamics and thermodynamics equations on 3D grids. While highly physical, these models are computationally intensive and slow, taking hours to generate a medium-range forecast.

Although AI models run much faster, they face their own design limitations:
1. **Autoregressive Error Accumulation**: To predict days in advance, models recursively feed their own output back as input. This autoregressive step requires encoding the physical variables into a latent space, rolling them forward, and decoding them back to physical space at every single time step. This constant encoding/decoding adds latency and accumulates spatial errors.
2. **Initial Condition Sensitivity**: Weather forecasts are highly dependent on initial conditions. Standard models ingest a single, clean analysis state. However, real-time analyses can have regional errors or delays. Blending multiple historical analyses and real-time observation fields is key to initializing accurate rollouts.
3. **Compute Accessibility**: Running global models at 0.25-degree resolution typically requires enterprise-level hardware (like A100/H100 GPUs), which restricts widespread adoption in resource-limited scenarios.

There is a clear need for an efficient, lightweight model architecture that can run medium-range forecasts on consumer hardware while maintaining operational accuracy.

## The Model and Approach

WeatherMesh-3 introduces two key architectural advances to solve these challenges:
1. **Latent Rollout**: Instead of decoding the latent state back to physical variables (like temperature, wind, pressure) at every intermediate time step, WM-3 performs the entire forecast rollout within the latent space. The model encodes the initial state once, rolls it forward for arbitrary lengths using latent-to-latent processors, and decodes only the final target forecast. This design avoids intermediate decoding overhead, minimizes error accumulation, and significantly accelerates inference speed.
2. **Modular Multi-Analysis Blender**: The model uses a modular processor architecture that can ingest and encode multiple real-time analyses and satellite observations simultaneously, blending them to create robust, error-resistant initial conditions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Multi-Analysis Input
        Analyses[Multiple Initial Analyses] --> Encoder[Encoder]
    end
    subgraph Latent Rollout
        Encoder -->|Latent State| Step1[Latent Step 1]
        Step1 -->|Latent State| Step2[Latent Step 2]
        Step2 -->|Latent State| StepN[Latent Step N]
    end
    subgraph Physical Output
        StepN --> Decoder[Decoder]
        Decoder --> Forecast[14-Day Global Forecast]
    end
    style Latent Rollout fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#34495e,stroke-width:2px;
```

WM-3 is optimized for consumer hardware, employing parameter-efficient attention mechanisms and weight quantization to fit within the memory limits of a single consumer GPU.

## Key Results and Benchmarks

WeatherMesh-3 was trained on historical ERA5 data and evaluated against operational numerical weather prediction and machine learning baselines:
* **Inference Speed**: WM-3 generated a full 14-day global weather forecast at 0.25-degree resolution in just **12 seconds** on a single consumer RTX 4090 GPU.
* **NWP Speedup**: This represents a **>100,000-fold speedup** over traditional physics-based NWP systems.
* **Accuracy Improvement**: Despite its lightweight design, WM-3 achieved superior accuracy over operational models, showing up to a **37.7% reduction in Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)** compared to conventional NWP forecasts.
* **Democratization**: By running on consumer-grade hardware, WM-3 opens the door for local weather agencies, shipping companies, and agricultural organizations to deploy custom, real-time global weather forecasting models locally.

These benchmarks show that lightweight, latent-space architectures can deliver high-accuracy operational forecasting without the need for supercomputing infrastructures.

## Key Takeaways
- **Latent Rollout Efficiency**: Performs multi-step predictions entirely in latent space, removing the computational cost and drift of intermediate decoding.
- **Consumer Hardware Friendly**: Runs 14-day global forecasts in 12 seconds on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU.
- **High Accuracy**: Outperforms operational physics-based models with up to a 37.7% improvement in RMSE.
- **Blended Initializations**: Modular input architecture integrates multiple real-time analyses for robust forecast initialization.

title: EPT-1.5 Technical Report
summary: We announce the release of EPT-1.5, the latest iteration in our Earth Physics Transformer (EPT) family of foundation AI earth system models. EPT-1.5 demonstrates substantial improvements over its predecessor, EPT-1. Built specifically for the European energy industry...
full_content: We announce the release of EPT-1.5, the latest iteration in our Earth Physics Transformer (EPT) family of foundation AI earth system models. EPT-1.5 demonstrates substantial improvements over its predecessor, EPT-1. Built specifically for the European energy industry, EPT-1.5 shows remarkable performance in predicting energy-relevant variables, particularly 10m & 100m wind speed and solar radiation. Especially in wind prediction, it outperforms existing AI weather models like GraphCast, FuXi, and Pangu-Weather, as well as the leading numerical weather model, IFS HRES by the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF), setting a new state of the art.
explanation:
The transition of European power grids to renewable energy sources has introduced high volatility into energy markets. Because wind and solar power generation are directly tied to local weather conditions, slight errors in wind speed or solar irradiance forecasts can translate to millions of Euros in grid balancing costs or trading losses. Traditional meteorological models, which focus on global atmospheric metrics, often fall short when predicting localized, surface-level weather parameters. To address these industry-specific needs, this technical report introduces EPT-1.5, a specialized version of the Earth Physics Transformer family designed specifically for the European energy industry, setting a new benchmark for wind and solar predictions.

## The Core Problem

For energy traders and grid operators, standard global weather forecasts have two major limitations:
1. **Vertical Resolution and Hub Heights**: Standard wind forecasts predict wind speed at 10 meters above the ground. However, modern commercial wind turbines operate at hub heights between 80 and 150 meters. Interpolating 10-meter winds up to hub heights using empirical laws (like the log wind profile) is highly inaccurate due to atmospheric stability variations and localized turbulence.
2. **Localized Surface Radiation**: Solar panels are sensitive to surface solar radiation, which is heavily influenced by cloud dynamics and aerosols. Physics-based numerical models solve cloud schemes using grid-scale approximations that often fail to resolve small-scale cloud cover, leading to systematic biases.
3. **Regional Optimization**: Global AI models (like GraphCast or Pangu-Weather) are trained with loss functions that weight the entire globe equally. Consequently, they optimize for broad atmospheric structures over oceans and large continents, rather than the localized topography and land-sea boundaries that drive weather across Europe.

To mitigate these issues, a model must be optimized specifically for European geography and boundary-layer physics, focusing directly on energy-relevant parameters.

## The Model and Approach

EPT-1.5 builds on the Earth Physics Transformer (EPT) architecture, incorporating several design modifications to meet the needs of the energy sector. The model operates on high-resolution atmospheric datasets covering the European continent, integrating historical ERA5 reanalysis with localized, real-time meteorological observations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph European Input Data
        Coarse[Coarse Atmospheric Fields] --> Enc[Spatial-Temporal Encoder]
        Local[Local European Observations] --> Enc
    end
    subgraph EPT-1.5 Transformer
        Enc --> Trans[Transformer Backbone]
        Trans --> CustomLoss[Energy-Weighted Loss Function]
    end
    subgraph Energy Outputs
        CustomLoss --> Wind100[100m Wind Speed]
        CustomLoss --> Wind10[10m Wind Speed]
        CustomLoss --> Solar[Surface Solar Radiation]
    end
```

The key innovations in EPT-1.5 include:
* **Energy-Weighted Loss**: The loss function is specifically weighted to penalize errors in boundary-layer wind speeds (10m and 100m) and solar radiation over the European grid.
* **Multi-Scale Feature Integration**: The transformer architecture incorporates local terrain elevations and land-use data as static inputs, allowing the model to learn how European topography (such as the Alps or coastal plains) affects wind patterns and cloud formation.
* **High Temporal Resolution**: EPT-1.5 outputs forecasts at short intervals, capturing the rapid wind ramps and solar cloud-shading events that drive electricity price spikes.

## Key Results and Benchmarks

EPT-1.5 was evaluated against leading AI weather models and traditional numerical weather prediction (NWP) systems:
* **Wind Speed Accuracy**: In wind forecasting (both at 10m and 100m turbine hub heights), EPT-1.5 outperformed leading AI weather models, including Google DeepMind's GraphCast, Huawei's Pangu-Weather, and FuXi.
* **NWP Comparison**: The model consistently outskilled the High-Resolution Integrated Forecasting System (IFS HRES) from the ECMWF, setting a new state-of-the-art performance benchmark for European wind energy grids.
* **Solar Radiation Forecasting**: EPT-1.5 demonstrated high skill in predicting surface solar radiation, reducing cloud-cover forecast errors and improving solar farm power output estimates.

These results highlight that regional and variable-specific optimization can yield significant performance gains over large, generalized global forecasting models.

## Key Takeaways
- **Targeted for Energy**: Specifically designed to predict 10m and 100m wind speeds and surface solar radiation for the European energy market.
- **Top-Tier Wind Skill**: Outperforms general AI models (GraphCast, FuXi, Pangu-Weather) in wind speed accuracy.
- **Surpasses IFS HRES**: Beats the leading European physical NWP model, providing a reliable tool for grid balancing and trading.
- **Topographic Awareness**: Integrates local topography and surface features to resolve boundary-layer atmospheric flows.

title: AIFS – ECMWF's data-driven forecasting system
summary: Machine learning-based weather forecasting models have quickly emerged as a promising methodology for accurate medium-range global weather forecasting. Here, we introduce the Artificial Intelligence Forecasting System (AIFS), a data driven forecast model developed by ECMWF...
full_content: Machine learning-based weather forecasting models have quickly emerged as a promising methodology for accurate medium-range global weather forecasting. Here, we introduce the Artificial Intelligence Forecasting System (AIFS), a data driven forecast model developed by the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF). AIFS is based on a graph neural network (GNN) encoder and decoder, and a sliding window transformer processor, and is trained on ECMWF's ERA5 re-analysis and ECMWF's operational numerical weather prediction (NWP) analyses. It has a flexible and modular design and supports several levels of parallelism to enable training on high-resolution input data. AIFS forecast skill is assessed by comparing its forecasts to NWP analyses and direct observational data. We show that AIFS produces highly skilled forecasts for upper-air variables, surface weather parameters and tropical cyclone tracks. AIFS is run four times daily alongside ECMWF's physics-based NWP model and forecasts are available to the public under ECMWF's open data policy.
explanation:
The European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) has long been considered the world leader in global weather forecasting, driven by its physics-based Integrated Forecasting System (IFS). However, the rapid rise of machine learning models trained on reanalysis data showed that data-driven approaches could match or exceed IFS accuracy at a fraction of the computational cost. Rather than treating AI as a competitor, ECMWF developed its own data-driven forecasting system: the Artificial Intelligence Forecasting System (AIFS). This paper introduces AIFS, a modular, scale-parallel system that combines Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with sliding window transformers to produce highly skilled operational forecasts.

## The Core Problem

Integrating machine learning into an operational, global weather forecasting pipeline faces several technical challenges:
1. **Global Spherical Geometry**: The Earth is a sphere, and projecting a spherical grid onto a flat 2D plane (like a standard image) introduces massive distortions at the poles. Models must process global atmospheric fields natively on a spherical surface.
2. **Computational Scale**: Operational forecasting requires training models on high-resolution data (like the 0.25-degree ERA5 dataset, which has over a million horizontal grid points and dozens of vertical levels). This requires massive GPU memory and highly parallelized training routines.
3. **Multi-Scale Physics**: Weather involves both local phenomena (like convective storm development) and large-scale atmospheric waves (like planetary Rossby waves). The model architecture must capture both local interactions and global teleconnections.
4. **Extreme Events**: Models must predict not just average weather states, but also critical high-impact events like tropical cyclone tracks, which require precise spatial tracking and physical consistency.

To address these challenges, ECMWF designed AIFS as a hybrid architecture capable of handling spherical grids and scaling across distributed GPU clusters.

## The Model and Approach

AIFS employs a hybrid Graph Neural Network (GNN) and Transformer architecture. The model is structured into three main blocks:
1. **GNN Encoder**: The encoder maps physical weather variables from the standard latitude-longitude grid onto a homogeneous, multi-mesh spherical graph (an icosahedron). This avoids pole distortions and represents the Earth's surface uniformly.
2. **Sliding Window Transformer**: Once the data is on the spherical mesh, a sliding window transformer processes the spatial relationships. The transformer architecture allows the model to learn long-range connections across the globe, while the sliding window constraint limits the attention computation to local neighborhoods, reducing memory usage.
3. **GNN Decoder**: The decoder maps the processed latent representations from the spherical mesh back to the standard latitude-longitude grid to output physical weather forecasts.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Spherical Ingestion
        Input[Lat-Lon Grid Inputs] --> GNN_Enc[GNN Encoder: Grid-to-Mesh]
    end
    subgraph Latent Processing
        GNN_Enc -->|Spherical Mesh| Trans[Sliding Window Transformer]
    end
    subgraph Grid Projection
        Trans -->|Spherical Mesh| GNN_Dec[GNN Decoder: Mesh-to-Grid]
        GNN_Dec --> Output[Global Forecasts & Cyclone Tracks]
    end
    style Latent Processing fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#34495e,stroke-width:2px;
```

AIFS is trained directly on ECMWF's ERA5 reanalysis and high-resolution operational analysis datasets, using advanced model-parallelism to scale training across multi-node GPU clusters.

## Key Results and Benchmarks

AIFS was integrated into ECMWF's operational suite, running four times daily in parallel with the physics-based IFS model:
* **Forecast Skill**: AIFS produces highly skilled forecasts for both upper-air variables (such as temperature, geopotential, and wind at various pressure levels) and surface parameters (such as 2m temperature and 10m wind speed).
* **Cyclone Tracking**: The model demonstrates exceptional capability in tracking tropical cyclone trajectories, matching or exceeding the accuracy of the physics-based IFS HRES.
* **Operational Integration**: Running AIFS takes under a minute on a single GPU, compared to the hours of supercomputing time required for the physics-based IFS.
* **Open Data Policy**: In line with ECMWF's commitment to open science, AIFS forecasts are published in real-time under an open data license, making state-of-the-art AI forecasts accessible to the global meteorological community.

This work marks the successful transition of AI-based weather prediction from research prototype to real-time operational utility at the world's premier weather agency.

## Key Takeaways
- **Hybrid GNN-Transformer**: Combines a spherical GNN encoder-decoder with a sliding window transformer processor to handle global atmospheric dynamics.
- **Operational Status**: Deployed and run four times daily in real-time at ECMWF alongside traditional NWP models.
- **Superior Cyclone Tracking**: Outperforms or matches physical models in tracking the paths of tropical cyclones.
- **Open Access**: Forecast data is fully open and publicly available, promoting global scientific collaboration.

title: A Foundation Model for the Earth System
summary: Reliable forecasts of the Earth system are crucial for human progress and safety from natural disasters. Artificial intelligence offers substantial potential to improve prediction accuracy and computational efficiency in this field... Here we introduce Aurora...
full_content: Reliable forecasts of the Earth system are crucial for human progress and safety from natural disasters. Artificial intelligence offers substantial potential to improve prediction accuracy and computational efficiency in this field, however this remains underexplored in many domains. Here we introduce Aurora, a large-scale foundation model for the Earth system trained on over a million hours of diverse data. Aurora outperforms operational forecasts for air quality, ocean waves, tropical cyclone tracks, and high-resolution weather forecasting at orders of magnitude smaller computational expense than dedicated existing systems. With the ability to fine-tune Aurora to diverse application domains at only modest computational cost, Aurora represents significant progress in making actionable Earth system predictions accessible to anyone.
explanation:
In artificial intelligence, foundation models (like GPT-4) have shown that training on massive, diverse datasets creates models that can generalize to tasks they were never explicitly trained for. Weather and climate forecasting, however, has traditionally relied on highly specialized, single-task models. A model trained for global weather forecasting cannot predict ocean waves or estimate air quality; separate, dedicated systems must be built for each task. This paper introduces Aurora, a large-scale foundation model for the Earth system developed by Microsoft. Trained on over a million hours of diverse atmospheric and climate data, Aurora can be fine-tuned to tackle multiple geoscientific domains—including high-resolution weather, air quality, ocean wave heights, and tropical cyclone tracks—at a fraction of the cost of dedicated systems.

## The Core Problem

The Earth system is a highly coupled network of physical subsystems: the atmosphere, the oceans, the biosphere, and the cryosphere. Traditional numerical weather prediction (NWP) models require separate codes and physical parameterizations for each component:
1. **Model Specialization**: The ECMWF runs the IFS for weather, CAMS for chemistry and air quality, and WAVEWATCH III for ocean waves. Each model requires separate development teams, supercomputers, and data pipelines.
2. **Data Heterogeneity**: Geoscientific data comes from diverse sources: reanalysis models, satellite sensors, weather stations, and ocean buoys. Standard machine learning models are designed for homogeneous datasets (e.g., only ERA5) and cannot ingest data with different resolutions, variables, or grids.
3. **Downstream Adaptability**: Developing a new forecasting model for a specific region or variable (e.g., air pollution in a specific city) requires starting from scratch, demanding massive computational budgets and years of historical data.

There is a critical need for an Earth system foundation model that learns general atmospheric representation and can adapt to diverse downstream geoscientific tasks.

## The Model and Approach

Aurora is built around a large-scale 3D Swin Transformer backbone, coupled with Perceiver-style encoders and decoders. This architecture allows Aurora to ingest heterogeneous, multi-resolution datasets. The model was pre-trained on over **1.3 million hours** of diverse atmospheric and climate model data from multiple sources: ERA5 reanalysis, climate model simulations (CMIP6), and operational analysis fields.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Multi-Dataset Input
        ERA5[ERA5 Reanalysis] --> Enc[Perceiver Encoder]
        CMIP6[CMIP6 Climate Data] --> Enc
        CAMS[CAMS Chemistry Data] --> Enc
    end
    subgraph Foundation Core
        Enc --> Swin3D[3D Swin Transformer Backbone]
        Swin3D --> Pretrain[Pre-trained on 1.3M Hours of Data]
    end
    subgraph Downstream Fine-Tuning
        Pretrain --> FT_Weather[High-Res Weather]
        Pretrain --> FT_AirQuality[CAMS Air Quality]
        Pretrain --> FT_Waves[Ocean Wave Heights]
    end
```

During pre-training, Aurora learns to predict the next atmospheric state, developing a deep understanding of atmospheric dynamics, radiative transfer, and chemistry. Once pre-trained, the foundation model can be fine-tuned for specific downstream tasks using small, specialized datasets. The Perceiver-style encoder-decoder maps arbitrary input grids and variables into a shared latent space, enabling the core transformer to process diverse datasets without changing its architecture.

## Key Results and Benchmarks

Microsoft Aurora was evaluated on a wide range of geoscientific tasks and compared against state-of-the-art operational baselines:
* **High-Resolution Weather**: Aurora outperformed the operational IFS HRES model on standard atmospheric variables, providing highly accurate medium-range forecasts.
* **Air Quality (CAMS)**: When fine-tuned for atmospheric chemistry, Aurora surpassed the operational Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS) in predicting air pollutants (such as nitrogen dioxide, sulfur dioxide, and particulate matter) at a fraction of the computational expense.
* **Ocean Waves**: Aurora was successfully fine-tuned to predict ocean wave heights, outperforming dedicated wave models and capturing the coupling between wind and sea surfaces.
* **Cyclone Tracks**: The model demonstrated high accuracy in tracking tropical cyclones, providing critical path predictions for disaster risk management.

By establishing that a single pre-trained model can adapt to weather, oceanography, and atmospheric chemistry, Aurora represents a major step forward in building generalist AI systems for Earth science.

## Key Takeaways
- **Earth Foundation Model**: Trained on over 1.3 million hours of diverse, multi-dataset climate and atmospheric observations.
- **Multitask Generalization**: Excels at weather forecasting, air quality estimation, ocean wave heights, and tropical cyclone tracking.
- **Heterogeneous Ingestion**: Uses Perceiver-style encoders to process variable formats, resolutions, and grids.
- **Low-Cost Fine-Tuning**: Can be adapted to complex downstream tasks with modest computational budgets, making high-quality forecasting accessible.

title: GenCast: Diffusion-based ensemble forecasting for medium-range weather
summary: Weather forecasts are fundamentally uncertain, so predicting the range of probable weather scenarios is crucial for important decisions... Here, we introduce GenCast, a probabilistic weather model with greater skill and speed than the top operational ensemble forecast...
full_content: Weather forecasts are fundamentally uncertain, so predicting the range of probable weather scenarios is crucial for important decisions, from warning the public about hazardous weather, to planning renewable energy use. Here, we introduce GenCast, a probabilistic weather model with greater skill and speed than the top operational medium-range weather forecast in the world, the European Centre for Medium-Range Forecasts (ECMWF)'s ensemble forecast, ENS. Unlike traditional approaches, which are based on numerical weather prediction (NWP), GenCast is a machine learning weather prediction (MLWP) method, trained on decades of reanalysis data. GenCast generates an ensemble of stochastic 15-day global forecasts, at 12-hour steps and 0.25 degree latitude-longitude resolution, for over 80 surface and atmospheric variables, in 8 minutes. It has greater skill than ENS on 97.4% of 1320 targets we evaluated, and better predicts extreme weather, tropical cyclones, and wind power production. This work helps open the next chapter in operational weather forecasting, where critical weather-dependent decisions are made with greater accuracy and efficiency.
explanation:
Weather forecasting is fundamentally probabilistic. The atmosphere is a chaotic system where small errors in the initial observations can grow exponentially over time—a phenomenon famously known as the "butterfly effect." To manage this uncertainty, operational meteorological centers run ensemble forecasts: they run their models dozens of times with slight variations in the initial conditions to produce a range of possible weather scenarios. However, traditional physical ensemble methods require massive supercomputers. Furthermore, deterministic AI weather models (which output a single prediction) tend to smooth out over time, failing to represent the true risk of extreme events. This paper introduces GenCast, a diffusion-based probabilistic weather model developed by Google DeepMind that generates skilled global ensemble forecasts in minutes on a single GPU.

## The Core Problem

Standard machine learning weather prediction (MLWP) models (like GraphCast or Pangu-Weather) are trained using deterministic loss functions (such as Mean Squared Error). While these models achieve low average errors, they face a major drawback:
1. **Spatial Blurring**: As the forecast lead time increases, the model's uncertainty grows. To minimize the average error, the model predicts the average of all possible futures. This leads to spatially smoothed forecasts where storm structures, front boundaries, and wind gusts are washed out.
2. **Ensemble Calibration**: Physical ensembles (like ECMWF ENS, which has 51 members) are computationally intensive, taking hours to run on supercomputers. Standard AI models do not natively output probabilities, and running them multiple times with random initial perturbations often leads to under-dispersed ensembles (they underestimate the range of possible futures).
3. **Extreme Weather Prediction**: Making critical decisions—like evacuating a coastline for a hurricane or scheduling wind power generation—requires knowing the probability of extreme events. Clean, smoothed forecasts fail to capture these high-impact tail risks.

There is a critical need for a generative AI model that can produce physically consistent, stochastically diverse ensemble forecasts that represent true atmospheric uncertainty.

## The Model and Approach

GenCast solves this problem by using a conditional denoising diffusion model. Instead of predicting the weather state directly, GenCast starts with random Gaussian noise and iteratively denoises it over several steps, conditioned on the recent atmospheric states (from ERA5 reanalysis).

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Conditioning Inputs
        State[Atmospheric State t-1, t] --> Gen[GenCast Diffusion Core]
    end
    subgraph Denoising Process
        Noise[Gaussian Noise] --> Gen
        Gen -->|Iterative Denoising| Member[Ensemble Member Sample]
    end
    subgraph Ensemble Generation
        Member -->|Run 50+ Times in Parallel| MultiMember[Full Probabilistic Ensemble]
    end
    style Ensemble Generation fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#34495e,stroke-width:2px;
```

By running this denoising process multiple times with different random noise seeds, GenCast generates an ensemble of distinct, physically consistent weather scenarios. The core processor is based on a multi-mesh Graph Neural Network (GNN) that operates on a spherical representation of the Earth. GenCast is trained on decades of ERA5 reanalysis data to output global forecasts at 0.25-degree resolution, predicting over 80 surface and atmospheric variables.

## Key Results and Benchmarks

GenCast was evaluated against the ECMWF ensemble system (ENS)—the world's leading operational physical ensemble model:
* **Forecast Skill**: Evaluated on 1,320 target variables and lead times, GenCast outperformed ECMWF ENS on **97.4%** of the targets, showing superior probabilistic accuracy (CRPS).
* **Extreme Events**: GenCast demonstrated excellent calibration for extreme weather events, accurately predicting heavy precipitation, temperature extremes, and the path and intensity of tropical cyclones.
* **Wind Power Calibration**: For renewable energy applications, GenCast provided well-calibrated wind speed probability distributions, allowing energy grid operators to manage wind power ramps and reserve capacity.
* **Inference Speed**: GenCast generated a 50-member, 15-day global forecast at 0.25-degree resolution in just **8 minutes** on a single Cloud TPU, representing a massive reduction in compute cost compared to physical NWP supercomputer runs.

GenCast marks the opening of a new chapter in meteorology, proving that diffusion-based generative models can deliver faster and more accurate probabilistic forecasts than conventional numerical ensembles.

## Key Takeaways
- **Diffusion-Based Ensembles**: Uses conditional denoising diffusion to generate stochastically diverse, physically realistic weather scenarios.
- **Beats the Gold Standard**: Outperforms the ECMWF ENS ensemble mean on 97.4% of evaluated targets.
- **Fast Probabilistic Inference**: Generates 15-day global ensembles in 8 minutes on a single device.
- **Extreme Weather Accuracy**: Provides superior calibration and tracking for hurricanes, wind ramps, and temperature extremes.

title: Scaling transformer neural networks for skillful and reliable medium-range weather forecasting
summary: Weather forecasting is a fundamental problem for anticipating and mitigating the impacts of climate change. Recently, data-driven approaches... have shown great promise. Here we introduce Stormer, a simple transformer model...
full_content: Weather forecasting is a fundamental problem for anticipating and mitigating the impacts of climate change. Recently, data-driven approaches for weather forecasting based on deep learning have shown great promise, achieving accuracies that are competitive with operational systems. However, those methods often employ complex, customized architectures without sufficient ablation analysis, making it difficult to understand what truly contributes to their success. Here we introduce Stormer, a simple transformer model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on weather forecasting with minimal changes to the standard transformer backbone. We identify the key components of Stormer through careful empirical analyses, including weather-specific embedding, randomized dynamics forecast, and pressure-weighted loss. At the core of Stormer is a randomized forecasting objective that trains the model to forecast the weather dynamics over varying time intervals. During inference, this allows us to produce multiple forecasts for a target lead time and combine them to obtain better forecast accuracy. On WeatherBench 2, Stormer performs competitively at short to medium-range forecasts and outperforms current methods beyond 7 days, while requiring orders-of-magnitude less training data and compute. Additionally, we demonstrate Stormer's favorable scaling properties, showing consistent improvements in forecast accuracy with increases in model size and training tokens. Code and checkpoints are available at https://github.com/tung-nd/stormer.
explanation:
The rapid development of deep learning models for weather forecasting has led to a proliferation of complex, custom architectures. Models often employ specialized spherical grids, nested graph neural networks, or custom attention blocks without detailed ablation analyses. This architectural complexity makes it difficult for researchers to understand what truly drives model performance: is it the custom graph geometry, the physical constraints, or simply the scale of the training data? This paper introduces Stormer, a model that simplifies the machine learning weather prediction landscape by utilizing a standard vision-style transformer backbone with minimal modifications, demonstrating outstanding performance through scaling and a novel training objective.

## The Core Problem

As AI weather forecasting matures, the research community faces three key challenges:
1. **Architectural Complexity**: Building and maintaining complex, specialized architectures (like multi-mesh GNNs) is difficult. They require custom GPU kernels, do not easily benefit from standard hardware optimizations, and are hard to scale using standard deep learning libraries.
2. **Autoregressive Drift**: Weather models are trained to predict the next step (e.g., 6 hours ahead). During inference, the model is run recursively to generate a 10-day forecast. This multi-step rollout accumulates errors, causing the model's predictions to drift away from physical reality at long lead times.
3. **Compute and Data Efficiency**: Training state-of-the-art weather models typically requires months of GPU time and decades of high-resolution climate data. Finding architectures that scale efficiently and work well with limited compute budgets is critical.

Stormer addresses these issues by demonstrating that standard transformer backbones, when paired with the right training objectives, can outperform complex custom architectures.

## The Model and Approach

Stormer is built on a standard Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone, making minimal changes to the core attention and feed-forward layers. Its success relies on three key design strategies:
1. **Weather-Specific Embedding**: Ingests atmospheric fields (temperature, wind, geopotential at multiple pressure levels) and projects them into spatial tokens, preserving vertical level correlations.
2. **Randomized Dynamics Forecasting**: Instead of training the model to predict a fixed time step (e.g., always t+6 hours), Stormer is trained to forecast weather dynamics over randomized time intervals (e.g., t+6h, t+12h, t+24h, etc.). This randomized objective forces the model to learn the underlying physical dynamics across multiple scales, stabilizing long-term rollouts.
3. **Pressure-Weighted Loss**: The loss function is weighted based on atmospheric pressure levels, prioritizing variables closer to the Earth's surface (where weather impacts human activity) while maintaining stability in the upper atmosphere.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Input State
        Input[Atmospheric State at t] --> Embed[Weather-Specific Embedding]
    end
    subgraph Transformer Core
        Embed --> ViT[Standard Vision Transformer Backbone]
        ViT --> Objective[Randomized Dynamics Forecast: dt = 6h, 12h, 24h]
    end
    subgraph Loss and Inference
        Objective --> Loss[Pressure-Weighted Loss]
        Loss --> Rollout[Inference Multi-Path Ensembling]
    end
```

During inference, the randomized dynamics capability allows Stormer to generate multiple forecast trajectories for a target lead time by combining different time-step combinations (e.g., predicting t+48h directly, or via two t+24h steps). Combining these paths produces a well-calibrated mean forecast.

## Key Results and Benchmarks

Stormer was evaluated on the WeatherBench 2 benchmark, the standard evaluation framework for global weather models:
* **Forecast Accuracy**: Stormer achieved competitive performance at short- to medium-range forecasts and outperformed existing state-of-the-art AI weather models beyond 7 days.
* **Compute and Data Efficiency**: The model achieved these results while requiring orders-of-magnitude less training data and computational resources compared to models like GraphCast.
* **Scaling Properties**: The authors demonstrated favorable scaling properties, showing consistent improvements in forecast accuracy as they increased model parameters and training tokens.
* **Open Source**: Code and checkpoints are available on GitHub, providing a simple, accessible baseline for the geoscientific machine learning community.

Stormer shows that standard, well-optimized transformer architectures can compete with and exceed specialized architectures, shifting the focus of AI meteorology toward scaling and training objectives.

## Key Takeaways
- **Standard Transformer Backbone**: Uses a standard Vision Transformer structure with minimal modifications, simplifying scaling.
- **Randomized Dynamics Forecast**: Training on variable time steps improves multi-step stability and reduces autoregressive drift.
- **Data & Compute Efficient**: Achieves competitive results with significantly less training data and compute than specialized models.
- **Favorable Scaling**: Demonstrates consistent performance gains as model parameters and tokens scale.

title: Neural General Circulation Models for Weather and Climate
summary: General circulation models (GCMs) are the foundation of weather and climate prediction... Recently, ML models achieved comparable or better skill... Here we present the first GCM that combines a differentiable solver for atmospheric dynamics with ML components...
full_content: General circulation models (GCMs) are the foundation of weather and climate prediction. GCMs are physics-based simulators which combine a numerical solver for large-scale dynamics with tuned representations for small-scale processes such as cloud formation. Recently, machine learning (ML) models trained on reanalysis data achieved comparable or better skill than GCMs for deterministic weather forecasting. However, these models have not demonstrated improved ensemble forecasts, or shown sufficient stability for long-term weather and climate simulations. Here we present the first GCM that combines a differentiable solver for atmospheric dynamics with ML components, and show that it can generate forecasts of deterministic weather, ensemble weather and climate on par with the best ML and physics-based methods. NeuralGCM is competitive with ML models for 1-10 day forecasts, and with the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts ensemble prediction for 1-15 day forecasts. With prescribed sea surface temperature, NeuralGCM can accurately track climate metrics such as global mean temperature for multiple decades, and climate forecasts with 140 km resolution exhibit emergent phenomena such as realistic frequency and trajectories of tropical cyclones. For both weather and climate, our approach offers orders of magnitude computational savings over conventional GCMs. Our results show that end-to-end deep learning is compatible with tasks performed by conventional GCMs, and can enhance the large-scale physical simulations that are essential for understanding and predicting the Earth system.
explanation:
For decades, global weather forecasting and climate projection have rested on General Circulation Models (GCMs)—physics-based simulators that solve the Navier-Stokes equations for atmospheric dynamics on global grids. Recently, pure machine learning (ML) models trained on reanalysis data (like ERA5) have emerged, outperforming GCMs in deterministic weather forecasting accuracy. However, these pure ML models face a major limitation: they do not solve physical equations directly, leading to physical drift and instability over long integration times (like seasonal or decadal climate simulations). This paper introduces NeuralGCM, a hybrid model developed by Google and research partners that combines a differentiable dynamical core (solving physical laws) with neural network parameterizations of small-scale processes, establishing a new paradigm for weather and climate modeling.

## The Core Problem

Traditional General Circulation Models (GCMs) split the atmosphere into two components:
1. **Dynamics**: Solving fluid dynamics equations for large-scale air movement. This is solved numerically using physical solvers.
2. **Physics (Parameterization)**: Representing small-scale processes that occur below the grid scale, such as cloud formation, radiative transfer, and turbulence. GCMs parameterize these processes using empirical, hand-tuned physical formulas.

While GCMs are physically consistent and stable over centuries, their parameterizations are a major source of error and bias.

Pure ML models replace both dynamics and physics with deep neural networks. While they are highly accurate for short-term forecasts (1-10 days), they suffer from critical drawbacks:
* **Physical Inconsistency**: Pure ML models can violate basic physical laws, such as conservation of mass, energy, and water vapor.
* **Long-Term Instability**: Over long rollouts (weeks to months), errors accumulate, and the model's atmospheric states diverge into physically impossible regimes.
* **Climate Emulation**: Because they lack physical constraints and long-term stability, pure ML models cannot be used for multi-decade climate change projections.

There is a clear need for a hybrid approach that combines the physical guarantees of a dynamical solver with the accuracy of deep learning parameterizations.

## The Model and Approach

NeuralGCM is a hybrid model that integrates a differentiable dynamical core with neural network parameterizations. The model is built on JAX, making the entire pipeline differentiable end-to-end.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Differentiable Solver
        Input[Atmospheric State] --> DynCore[Physical Dynamical Core: Solves Fluid Equations]
    end
    subgraph Deep Learning Parameterization
        DynCore -->|Large-Scale Flow| ML_Param[Neural Network Parameterization]
        ML_Param -->|Sub-Grid Physics Tendencies| DynCore
    end
    subgraph Output Forecast
        DynCore --> StableOut[Stable Multi-Decade Climate Simulation]
    end
    style Differentiable Solver fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#34495e,stroke-width:2px;
```

The workflow operates as follows:
1. The **differentiable physical solver** updates the large-scale atmospheric variables (such as wind velocity, temperature, and pressure) using standard physical equations.
2. The **neural network parameterization** (a neural network trained end-to-end) takes the dynamical state and estimates the tendencies (changes) caused by small-scale physical processes like cloud formation and precipitation.
3. The model feeds these tendencies back into the dynamical solver to update the global state.

By keeping the dynamical core physical, the model guarantees the conservation of mass and momentum, while the neural network learns to represent complex sub-grid physics directly from high-resolution observations (ERA5).

## Key Results and Benchmarks

NeuralGCM was evaluated on both short-term weather forecasting and multi-decade climate simulation scales:
* **Deterministic Weather**: For 1-10 day forecasts, NeuralGCM is competitive with the best pure ML models and outperforms traditional physical GCMs.
* **Ensemble Forecasting**: NeuralGCM generates well-calibrated ensemble forecasts, matching the performance of the ECMWF ensemble system (ENS) for 1-15 day horizons.
* **Climate Stability**: Unlike pure ML models, NeuralGCM is stable over decades. When run with prescribed sea surface temperatures, it accurately tracked global mean temperatures and moisture metrics for over **40 years** without drift.
* **Emergent Phenomena**: In long-term simulations, NeuralGCM reproduced realistic weather statistics, including the emergent frequency and seasonal trajectories of tropical cyclones.
* **Compute Savings**: NeuralGCM offers orders-of-magnitude computational savings over conventional GCMs, running at a fraction of the hardware cost.

NeuralGCM demonstrates that physics and machine learning are not mutually exclusive, providing a template for the next generation of climate simulators.

## Key Takeaways
- **Hybrid Architecture**: Combines a differentiable JAX-based physical dynamical core with neural network sub-grid parameterization.
- **Physical Guarantees**: Guarantees conservation of mass and momentum, ensuring physical consistency during simulations.
- **Multi-Decade Stability**: Remains stable and realistic over 40+ years of climate simulation, avoiding the drift of pure ML models.
- **Low Compute Overhead**: Runs orders of magnitude faster than conventional physics-based GCMs, making high-resolution climate modeling accessible.

title: FuXi: A cascade machine learning forecasting system for 15-day global weather forecast
summary: Over the past few years, ML models have shown superior performance... However, the challenge remains to perform comparably to the ECMWF ensemble mean (EM) in 15-day forecasts. Therefore, we present FuXi, a cascaded ML weather forecasting system...
full_content: Over the past few years, due to the rapid development of machine learning (ML) models for weather forecasting, state-of-the-art ML models have shown superior performance compared to the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF)'s high-resolution forecast (HRES) in 10-day forecasts at a spatial resolution of 0.25 degree. However, the challenge remains to perform comparably to the ECMWF ensemble mean (EM) in 15-day forecasts. Previous studies have demonstrated the importance of mitigating the accumulation of forecast errors for effective long-term forecasts. Despite numerous efforts to reduce accumulation errors, including autoregressive multi-time step loss, using a single model is found to be insufficient to achieve optimal performance in both short and long lead times. Therefore, we present FuXi, a cascaded ML weather forecasting system that provides 15-day global forecasts with a temporal resolution of 6 hours and a spatial resolution of 0.25 degree. FuXi is developed using 39 years of the ECMWF ERA5 reanalysis dataset. The performance evaluation, based on latitude-weighted root mean square error (RMSE) and anomaly correlation coefficient (ACC), demonstrates that FuXi has comparable forecast performance to ECMWF EM in 15-day forecasts, making FuXi the first ML-based weather forecasting system to accomplish this achievement.
explanation:
In the field of machine learning-based weather prediction, models have achieved remarkable success in short- to medium-range forecasting (0 to 10 days). However, extending these forecasts to the 15-day horizon remains a major challenge. The difficulty lies in the recursive nature of forecasting: because models feed their own predictions back as inputs for the next step, small spatial errors and coordinate misalignments accumulate. Over a 15-day rollout, this error accumulation causes standard models to undergo spatial smoothing, resulting in blurry, unrealistic forecasts that lose meteorologically significant details. This paper introduces FuXi, a cascaded machine learning weather forecasting system that achieves high-accuracy 15-day global forecasts by using three separate models optimized for different lead-time horizons.

## The Core Problem

Autoregressive machine learning weather prediction models are trained to map the state of the atmosphere at time `t` to `t + dt` (e.g., 6 hours ahead). During inference, this step is repeated recursively to generate a long-term forecast.

This approach suffers from two primary issues:
1. **Error Accumulation**: Any prediction error at step 1 becomes an input error for step 2. Over dozens of steps, these errors grow, leading to physical drift and spatial blurring.
2. **Horizon Trade-Offs**: A model optimized to predict short-term weather (e.g., 1-5 days) requires high-frequency features. However, using these same settings for long-term forecasts (e.g., 10-15 days) leads to rapid instability and noise amplification. Conversely, a model optimized for long-term averages will produce overly smooth, inaccurate forecasts in the short term.

Using a single neural network with a single set of weights is insufficient to achieve optimal forecasting performance across both short and long lead times.

## The Model and Approach

FuXi solves the error accumulation problem by introducing a **cascaded forecasting system**. Instead of using a single model for the entire 15-day rollout, FuXi utilizes three distinct deep neural networks, each optimized for a specific forecast horizon:
1. **FuXi-Short**: Optimized to forecast lead times from **0 to 5 days**. It focuses on retaining high-resolution atmospheric details and rapid changes.
2. **FuXi-Medium**: Optimized for lead times from **5 to 10 days**. It balances detail retention with spatial consistency, mapping intermediate atmospheric flows.
3. **FuXi-Long**: Optimized for lead times from **10 to 15 days**. It is trained with a loss function that penalizes long-term error drift, keeping the system stable at the end of the rollout.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Input State
        Init[Initial Atmosphere State] --> Short[FuXi-Short Model: 0-5 days]
    end
    subgraph Cascade Pipeline
        Short -->|t = 5 days| Med[FuXi-Medium Model: 5-10 days]
        Med -->|t = 10 days| Long[FuXi-Long Model: 10-15 days]
    end
    subgraph Final Forecast
        Long --> Output[15-Day Global Forecast: 6h intervals]
    end
    style Cascade Pipeline fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#34495e,stroke-width:2px;
```

Each component of the cascade is built using a U-Net style transformer architecture trained on 39 years of ECMWF ERA5 reanalysis data. During inference, the models hand off the atmospheric state seamlessly, passing the latent states forward to generate a 15-day global forecast at 0.25-degree resolution with 6-hour intervals.

## Key Results and Benchmarks

FuXi was evaluated using standard meteorological metrics, including latitude-weighted Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) and Anomaly Correlation Coefficient (ACC):
* **Ensemble Mean Comparison**: FuXi became the first machine learning-based weather forecasting system to match the performance of the ECMWF Ensemble Mean (EM) in 15-day global forecasts.
* **Spatial Realism**: The cascaded design successfully mitigated spatial smoothing, allowing the model to retain physical structures (like pressure fronts and jet stream waves) even at 15-day lead times.
* **Extreme Weather Skill**: The system demonstrated high accuracy in tracking tropical cyclones and predicting extreme temperature changes, outperforming traditional physics-based deterministic models.

FuXi proves that cascading multiple specialized networks is an effective strategy for overcoming error accumulation in long-term machine learning weather prediction.

## Key Takeaways
- **Cascaded Architecture**: Uses three lead-time-specific models (Short, Medium, Long) to optimize forecasting across a 15-day horizon.
- **Matches ECMWF EM**: The first AI forecasting system to achieve performance comparable to the ECMWF Ensemble Mean at 15 days.
- **Reduced Blurring**: Mitigates spatial smoothing and autoregressive drift, retaining sharp meteorological structures.
- **ERA5 Foundation**: Trained on 39 years of global atmospheric reanalysis data, capturing long-term climate dynamics.

title: ClimaX: A foundation model for weather and climate
summary: Most state-of-the-art approaches... are based on physics-informed numerical models. We develop and demonstrate ClimaX, a flexible and generalizable deep learning model for weather and climate science that can be trained using heterogeneous datasets...
full_content: Most state-of-the-art approaches for weather and climate modeling are based on physics-informed numerical models of the atmosphere. These approaches aim to model the non-linear dynamics and complex interactions between multiple variables, which are challenging to approximate. Additionally, many such numerical models are computationally intensive, especially when modeling the atmospheric phenomenon at a fine-grained spatial and temporal resolution. Recent data-driven approaches based on machine learning instead aim to directly solve a downstream forecasting or projection task by learning a data-driven functional mapping using deep neural networks. However, these networks are trained using curated and homogeneous climate datasets for specific spatiotemporal tasks, and thus lack the generality of numerical models. We develop and demonstrate ClimaX, a flexible and generalizable deep learning model for weather and climate science that can be trained using heterogeneous datasets spanning different variables, spatio-temporal coverage, and physical groundings. ClimaX extends the Transformer architecture with novel encoding and aggregation blocks that allow effective use of available compute while maintaining general utility.
